item.title: Rare Earths, Reimagined: How AI Is Learning to Design New Magnets Before the Lab Work Begins
item.body:
For decades, the world's most powerful permanent magnets have depended on a handful of hard-to-source elements — neodymium, dysprosium, samarium — collectively known as rare earths. They sit inside electric vehicle motors, wind turbine generators, smartphone speakers, and countless industrial machines. They are also a well-known supply chain headache: mining them is environmentally costly, refining them is concentrated in a few countries, and demand keeps climbing.

That is why a recent line of research from Ames National Laboratory in Iowa has drawn so much attention. Scientists there are using physics-informed artificial intelligence to search for permanent magnet materials that need **no rare earth elements at all** — and to do much of that searching on a computer, before anyone melts a single ingot in a lab.

It is a genuinely exciting development. It is also one that deserves careful framing, because some headlines have run ahead of what the science actually says. Here is what is really happening, and why it matters.

## The problem with rare earths

"Rare earth" is a slightly misleading name. These 17 elements are not truly rare in the Earth's crust, but they rarely occur in concentrated, easily mined deposits. Extracting and separating them requires energy-intensive chemical processing that produces significant waste.

The strongest commercial magnets today — neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) magnets — rely on these elements to achieve their remarkable combination of magnetic strength and stability at high temperatures. A single electric vehicle can contain a few kilograms of rare earth magnets; a large offshore wind turbine can use hundreds of kilograms or more.

Finding a magnet that performs comparably without rare earths has been a goal of materials science for decades. The difficulty is the sheer size of the search space: the number of possible alloy combinations, crystal structures, and processing routes is astronomically large. Traditional discovery — synthesize a candidate, test it, adjust, repeat — can take years for a single promising material.

## What Ames Laboratory is actually doing

In work published in 2026, Ames Lab scientist Prashant Singh and colleagues laid out an **AI-driven roadmap** for designing next-generation permanent magnets. The approach combines three ingredients:

- **Physics-based modeling**, which describes how a material's magnetic behavior emerges from its atomic and electronic structure
- **High-throughput simulations**, which allow thousands of candidate compositions to be evaluated computationally
- **Reasoning-based AI tools**, which help narrow the enormous search space toward candidates most likely to succeed

The goal is to predict key magnetic properties — strength, stability, resistance to demagnetization — directly from a material's fundamental structure, so that only the most promising candidates ever need to be synthesized and tested in a physical lab.

This work builds on an earlier AI system Singh developed called **DuctGPT**. Contrary to some reporting, DuctGPT was not built to invent magnets. It was designed to predict *ductility* — how well a metal deforms without breaking — in refractory alloys used for demanding applications like fusion energy and aerospace. What made DuctGPT notable was its design philosophy: rather than acting as a black box that memorizes patterns in data, it was built to incorporate the underlying physics of materials, making its predictions more interpretable and more trustworthy when extrapolating to compositions no one has tested before.

That same philosophy — AI that reasons *with* physics rather than around it — is now being extended toward the magnet problem.

## Why "physics-informed" AI is the real story

Most machine learning systems in materials science learn from databases of known materials. That works well for interpolation — finding materials similar to ones we already know — but it struggles with true novelty. If a revolutionary magnet exists in an unexplored corner of chemical space, a pattern-matching model trained only on familiar compounds may never point there.

Physics-informed AI takes a different route. By encoding established physical laws and quantum-mechanical relationships into the model, researchers give the AI a kind of scientific intuition. It does not just know *that* certain alloys are magnetic; it has representations of *why* — how electron interactions, crystal symmetry, and atomic composition produce magnetism in the first place.

This matters for three reasons:

1. **Novelty becomes reachable.** A model grounded in physics can make credible predictions about compositions that appear in no existing database.
2. **Predictions become explainable.** Researchers can interrogate why the model favors a candidate, which builds the trust needed to invest in expensive synthesis experiments.
3. **Failures become informative.** When a physics-informed prediction misses, the discrepancy itself teaches scientists something about the material.

## Separating the breakthrough from the hype

It is worth being precise here, because several viral headlines have suggested that an AI has already "invented" a working rare-earth-free magnet that can replace NdFeB. That is not what the Ames Lab research claims.

What exists today is a **methodology and a roadmap**: a validated framework for using physics-informed AI to identify promising rare-earth-free candidates far faster than trial-and-error allows. Candidate materials flagged by these tools still must be synthesized, characterized, and tested — and history shows that many computational candidates fall short in the real world, whether due to manufacturing difficulties, thermal instability, or cost.

Independent analysts have made the same point: no demonstrated drop-in replacement for rare earth magnets has been announced. The honest summary is that AI has dramatically improved the *map*, not that the *destination* has been reached.

That said, improving the map is no small thing. If computational screening can eliminate 99% of dead ends before lab work begins, the pace of materials discovery could shift from decades to years. Similar AI-guided approaches elsewhere in the field — including efforts by national labs, universities, and startups — suggest the whole discipline is moving in this direction.

## What it could mean if it works

The stakes are larger than any single material. Permanent magnets sit at the heart of the energy transition: electric motors, generators, robotics, and grid infrastructure all depend on them. A viable rare-earth-free magnet would:

- **Diversify supply chains**, reducing dependence on geographically concentrated mining and refining
- **Lower environmental costs**, since rare earth extraction is among the more polluting corners of the minerals industry
- **Stabilize prices** for EVs, wind power, and consumer electronics, which are all exposed to rare earth market volatility

And beyond magnets, the deeper implication is about *how science gets done*. If physics-informed AI can reliably propose novel materials — not just remix known ones — then computation becomes a genuine engine of discovery rather than a filtering tool. The lab bench does not disappear; it becomes the place where the best ideas are confirmed rather than where all ideas begin.

## The bottom line

Ames National Laboratory has not conjured a miracle magnet out of a chatbot. What its researchers have done is arguably more important for the long run: they have shown a credible, physics-grounded path for AI to explore the vast space of possible materials and point human scientists toward the candidates most worth building.

The rare-earth-free magnet the world needs may still be years away. But for the first time, the search for it looks less like wandering in the dark and more like navigation — with AI holding a map drawn from the laws of physics themselves.
